title: Ezekiel 10
subtitle: The Glory Departs from the Temple

sections.header: Verses 1-8 — Coals of Fire from Among the Cherubim

heb.paragraph: The man in linen takes burning coals from between the cherubim and scatters them over the city. These are not ordinary coals but fire from the divine presence — the same holiness that purified Isaiah's lips (Isa 6:6-7) now becomes an instrument of destruction. Holy fire can cleanse or consume, depending on the condition of those it touches.

refs.note: A seraph took a coal from the altar to purify Isaiah's lips — the same heavenly fire that purifies can also destroy.
The LORD rained down burning sulfur on Sodom — fire from heaven as instrument of divine judgment.

notes.note: The sapphire throne above the cherubim connects this vision to chapter 1 — the same throne, now positioned over the temple, about to depart.
Fire scattered over the city from the divine throne means Jerusalem's destruction comes directly from God's presence, not merely from Babylonian military action.
The glory moves from above the cherubim to the threshold — a dramatic pause. God lingers at the doorway as if reluctant to leave, giving one final moment before departure.
Calvin interprets the coals as demonstrating that Jerusalem's burning is not mere military catastrophe but divine judgment executed from heaven. The Babylonians are instruments; God is the agent.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The 'whirling wheels' (galgal) may be a distinct term from the ophannim of chapter 1, though the two are used interchangeably. The term suggests spinning motion.
The cloud filling the court recalls the cloud that filled the tabernacle (Exod 40:34-35) and Solomon's temple (1 Kgs 8:10-11) — the glory that once came to dwell now prepares to depart.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block stresses the christological significance: the fire that destroys Jerusalem comes from between the cherubim — from the mercy seat itself. The place of atonement becomes the source of destruction. When mercy is persistently rejected, the throne of grace becomes the throne of judgment.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli argues that chapter 10 is a deliberate literary recapitulation of chapter 1, now identifying the hayyot as kerubim. This identification links the mobile throne-chariot to the stationary ark-cherubim of the temple, unifying the heavenly and earthly thrones.

hist.context: Chapter 10 parallels and identifies the living creatures of chapter 1 as cherubim — the throne-guardians of the ark. The chapter serves a dual function: it describes the mechanism of Jerusalem's destruction (fire from the divine throne) and narrates the first stage of the glory's departure. The glory moves from above the cherubim to the threshold of the temple — a partial withdrawal, a last pause before full departure.

sections.header: Verses 9-22 — The Glory Lifts from the Threshold to the East Gate

heb.paragraph: The kavod YHWH now moves in stages: from above the cherubim to the threshold (v.4), then from the threshold to the east gate of the temple (v.19). This staged departure is theologically significant — God does not flee; he withdraws deliberately, step by step, as if giving Jerusalem every chance to repent before the final departure in 11:22-23.

refs.note: Ichabod — 'the glory has departed from Israel' — when the ark was captured by the Philistines. Ezekiel narrates a far more devastating departure.
The glory will return from the east — the same direction it departed. What chapters 10-11 take away, chapter 43 will restore.
Jesus laments over Jerusalem: 'Your house is left to you desolate' — echoing the glory's departure.

notes.note: The glory moves from the threshold to the east gate — still within the temple complex but moving toward the exit. God's departure is gradual, reluctant, and painful.
Ezekiel explicitly identifies the living creatures of chapter 1 as cherubim. The visionary and priestly traditions converge: the chariot-throne of the visionary is the ark-throne of the priest.

Calvin reads the gradual departure as divine patience: God does not abandon suddenly but withdraws step by step, each pause an implicit invitation to repentance. When repentance does not come, the next step of withdrawal follows.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The first face is called a 'cherub' face here instead of the 'ox' face of 1:10 — this may indicate that the ox face is the characteristic face of the cherub, or it may reflect a textual variant.
The east gate faces the Mount of Olives — the glory departs toward the east, the direction from which it will return in 43:1-4.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block sees the departure as the theological center of the entire book: everything before chapter 10 explains why the glory must leave; everything after anticipates its return. The kavod theology structures the whole book: presence (ch. 1), departure (chs. 10-11), absence (chs. 12-39), return (ch. 43).

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli notes that the departure moves eastward — toward Babylon, toward the exiles. The glory does not ascend and vanish but travels horizontally, following the exiles. God's departure from Jerusalem is simultaneously his arrival among the deportees.

hist.context: The detailed re-description of the cherubim (four faces, four wings, wheels full of eyes) serves to identify them with the living creatures of chapter 1. The theological point is profound: the God who appeared in Babylon (ch. 1) is the same God now departing Jerusalem (ch. 10). The throne is mobile — it came to Babylon before it left Jerusalem, ensuring the exiles were never without divine presence.

rows.text: Command: Coals of Fire over the City

rows.label: vv.3-8
rows.text: The Glory Moves to the Threshold

rows.label: vv.9-17
rows.text: Re-description of the Cherubim and Wheels

rows.label: vv.18-22
rows.text: The Glory Departs to the East Gate

lit.note: The theological pivot of Ezekiel: the glory departs the temple in stages. The cherubim of chapter 1 are identified as the temple cherubim, unifying vision and liturgy.

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks at 10: God controls every stage of his own departure. Holiness at 9: the glory departs because the temple has been defiled beyond recovery.

hebtext.note: The man in linen takes burning coals from between the cherubim and scatters them over the city. These are not ordinary coals but fire from the divine presence — the same holiness that purified Isaiah's lips (Isa 6:6-7) now becomes an instrument of destruction. Holy fire can cleanse or consume, depending on the condition of those it touches.

The kavod YHWH now moves in stages: from above the cherubim to the threshold (v.4), then from the threshold to the east gate of the temple (v.19). This staged departure is theologically significant — God does not flee; he withdraws deliberately, step by step, as if giving Jerusalem every chance to repent before the final departure in 11:22-23.

ppl.name: Ezekiel
ppl.text: Ezekiel explicitly identifies the living creatures of chapter 1 as cherubim…

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Glory Departs from the Temple

translations.text: I looked, and I saw the likeness of a throne of lapis lazuli above the vault that was over the heads of the cherubim.
Then I looked, and behold, on the expanse that was over the heads of the cherubim there appeared above them something like a sapphire, in appearance like a throne.
I looked, and I saw beside the cherubim four wheels, one beside each of the cherubim; the wheels sparkled like topaz.
And I looked, and behold, there were four wheels beside the cherubim, one beside each cherub, and the appearance of the wheels was like sparkling beryl.

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "The sapphire throne above the cherubim connects this vision to chapter 1 — the same throne, now positioned over the temple, about to depart."

rec.title: John Calvin
rec.quote: "Calvin interprets the coals as demonstrating that Jerusalem's burning is not mere military catastrophe but divine judgment executed from heaven. The Babylonians are instruments; God is the agent."

rec.title: NET Bible translators
rec.quote: "The 'whirling wheels' (galgal) may be a distinct term from the ophannim of chapter 1, though the two are used interchangeably. The term suggests spinning motion."

thread.text: Jesus laments over Jerusalem: 'Your house is left to you desolate' — echoing the glory's departure.

coaching.tip: Coals of fire are taken from between the cherubim and scattered over the city (v. 2). In Isaiah 6, a coal from the altar purifies; in Ezekiel 10, coals from the cherubim destroy. Same source, opposite function. Fire that cleanses the repentant consumes the defiant. The image carries a warning: the presence of God is either salvation or judgment depending on the posture of the one who encounters it.

The glory of the LORD moves from the inner sanctuary to the threshold (9:3), from the threshold to the east gate (10:19), and will depart to the Mount of Olives in 11:23. The departure is gradual — reluctant, stage by stage, as if God is giving every chance for repentance before leaving. The glory doesn't flee; it withdraws, pausing at each exit. The slowness is mercy.
